analogy: Think of "Movements of Ocean Water" through the chapter's own opening images: FUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 114 LIFE ON THE EARTH This unit deals with • Biosphere — biodiversity and conservation UNIT VI BIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION You have already learnt about the geomorphic processes particularly weathering and depth of weathering mantle in different climatic zones. See the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LIFE ON THE EARTH, BIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION, BIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION 117, LOSS OF BIODIVERSITY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Movements of Ocean Water is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 114 LIFE ON THE EARTH This unit deals with • Biosphere — biodiversity and conservation UNIT VI

The text explains that bIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION You have already learnt about the geomorphic processes particularly weathering and depth of weathering mantle in different climatic zones.

The text explains that see the Figure 5.2 in Chapter 5 in order to recapitulate.

The text explains that you should know that this weathering mantle is the basis for the diversity of vegetation and hence, the biodiversity.

The text explains that the basic cause for such weathering variations and resultant biodiversity is the input of solar energy and water.

The text explains that no wonder that the areas that are rich in these inputs are the areas of wide spectrum of biodiversity.

The text explains that biodiversity as we have today is the result of 2.5-3.5 billion years of evolution.

The text explains that before the advent of humans, our earth supported more biodiversity than in any other period.

The text explains that since, the emergence of humans, however, biodiversity has begun a rapid decline, with one species after another bearing the brunt of extinction due to overuse.

The text explains that the number of species globally vary from 2 million to 100 million, with 10 million being the best estimate.

The text explains that new species are regularly discovered most of which are yet to be classified (an estimate states that about 40 per cent of fresh water fishes from South America are not classified yet).

The text explains that tropical forests are very rich in bio-diversity.

The text explains that biodiversity is a system in constant evolution, from a view point of species, as well as from view point of an individual organism.

The text explains that the average half-life of a species is estimated at between one and four million years, and 99 per cent of the species that have ever lived on the earth are today extinct.

The text explains that biodiversity is not found evenly on the earth.

The text explains that as one approaches the polar regions, one finds larger and larger populations of fewer and fewer species.

The text explains that it refers to the varieties of plants, animals and micro-organisms, the genes they contain and the ecosystems they form.

The text explains that it relates to the variability among living organisms on the earth, including the variability within and between the species and that within and between the ecosystems.

The text explains that it is a result of hundreds of millions of years of evolutionary history.

The text explains that biodiversity can be discussed at three levels : (i) Genetic diversity; (ii) Species diversity; (iii) Ecosystem diversity.

The text explains that ecosystem Diversity You have studied about the ecosystem in the earlier chapter.

The text explains that the broad differences between ecosystem types and the diversity of habitats and ecological processes occurring within each ecosystem type constitute the ecosystem diversity.

The text explains that the ‘boundaries’ of communities (associations of species) and ecosystems are not very rigidly defined.

The text explains that thus, the demarcation of ecosystem boundaries is difficult and complex. ecosystem evolves and sustains without any reason.

The text explains that can you think of the way we, humans contribute to the sustenance of ecosystems.

The text explains that species capture and store energy, produce and decompose organic materials, help to cycle water and nutrients throughout the ecosystem, fix atmospheric gases and help regulate the climate.

The text explains that these functions are important for ecosystem function and human survival.

The text explains that the more diverse an ecosystem, better are the chances for the species to survive through adversities and attacks, and consequently, is more productive.

The text explains that hence, the loss of species would decrease the ability of the system to maintain itself.

The text explains that just like a species with a high genetic diversity, an ecosystem with high biodiversity may have a greater chance of adapting to environmental change.

The text explains that in other words, the more the variety of species in an ecosystem, the more stable the ecosystem is likely to be.

The text explains that economic Role of Biodiversity For all humans, biodiversity is an important resource in their day-to-day life.

The text explains that biodiversity is seen as a reservoir of resources to be drawn upon for the manufacture of food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic products.

The text explains that this concept of biological resources is responsible for the deterioration of biodiversity.

The text explains that at the same time, it is also the origin of new conflicts dealing with rules of division and appropriation of natural resources.

The text explains that some of the important economic commodities that biodiversity supplies to humankind are: food crops, livestock, forests, fish, medicinal resources, etc.

The text explains that ecological Role of Biodiversity Species of many kinds perform some function or the other in an ecosystem.

The text explains that nothing in an Figure 14.1 : Grasslands and sholas in Indira Gandhi National Park, Annamalai, Western Ghats — an example of ecosystem diversity

The text explains that bIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION 117 ecosystems of which we are also a species.

The text explains that it is our ethical responsibility to consider that each and every species along with us have an intrinsic right to exist.

The text explains that lOSS OF BIODIVERSITY Since the last few decades, growth in human population has increased the rate of consumption of natural resources.

The text explains that over- exploitation of resources and deforestation have become rampant to fulfil the needs of large population.

The text explains that as these tropical rain forests contain 50 per cent of the species on the earth, destruction of natural habitats have proved disastrous for the entire biosphere.

The text explains that natural calamities such as earthquakes, floods, volcanic eruptions, forest fires, droughts, etc. cause damage to the flora and fauna of the earth, bringing change the biodiversity of respective affected regions.

The text explains that (iii) Varieties of food crops, forage plants, timber trees, livestock, animals and their wild relatives should be preserved; Figure 14.3 : Humbodtia decurrens Bedd — highly rare endemic tree of Southern Western Ghats (India)

The text explains that bIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION 119 Figure 14.4 : Some ecological ‘hotspots’ in the world EXERCISES 1.

The text explains that (i) Conservation of biodiversity is important for : (a) Animals (c) Plants (b) Animals and plants (d) All organisms (ii) Threatened species are those which : (a) threaten others (b) Lion and tiger (c) are abundant in number (d) are suffering from the danger of extinction (iii) National parks and sanctuaries are established for the purpose of : (a) Recreation (c) Pets (b) Hunting (d) Conservation

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 120 (iv) Biodiversity is richer in : (a) Tropical Regions (c) Temperate Regions (b) Polar Regions (d) Oceans (v) In which one of the following countries, the ‘Earth Summit’ was held?

The text explains that (a) the UK (c) Brazil (b) Mexico (d) China 2.

The text explains that (ii) What are the different levels of biodiversity?

The text explains that (iv) Discuss briefly the importance of animals to human kind.

The text explains that (v) What do you understand by ‘exotic species’?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 14: Movements of Ocean Water

revision_bullets: FUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 114 LIFE ON THE EARTH This unit deals with • Biosphere — biodiversity and conservation UNIT VI (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)

BIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION You have already learnt about the geomorphic processes particularly weathering and depth of weathering mantle in different climatic zones. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
See the Figure 5.2 in Chapter 5 in order to recapitulate. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
You should know that this weathering mantle is the basis for the diversity of vegetation and hence, the biodiversity. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
The basic cause for such weathering variations and resultant biodiversity is the input of solar energy and water. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
No wonder that the areas that are rich in these inputs are the areas of wide spectrum of biodiversity. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
Biodiversity as we have today is the result of 2.5-3.5 billion years of evolution. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
Before the advent of humans, our earth supported more biodiversity than in any other period. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
Since, the emergence of humans, however, biodiversity has begun a rapid decline, with one species after another bearing the brunt of extinction due to overuse. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)
The number of species globally vary from 2 million to 100 million, with 10 million being the best estimate. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch14 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LIFE ON THE EARTH and BIODIVERSITY AND CONSERVATION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Movements of Ocean Water; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
